schools.summary: Gill: as care was taken for the holiness of all Israel, the priests who served God especially must keep from all defilement; the section follows the law on familiar spirits, teaching men to seek the priest in doubt, not the diviner.

themes: Speak unto the priests the sons of Aaron
none be defiled for the dead
the priest's stricter holiness (Heb. 7:26)

schools.summary: Gill: lists the near kin for whom the priest might be defiled and mourn; the opening clause ('his flesh') is taken to include his wife, his own flesh, the nearest of all, though not elsewhere expressed.

themes: for his kin, that is near unto him
his mother, and his father, and his son
natural affection ordered, not extinguished

schools.summary: Gill: takes the nearness as of place as well as kin, the unmarried sister remaining in her father's house till death, for whom the priest might be defiled.
Ellicott: the maiden sister still in sole relationship with him, who when married would go to her husband's house, is included among those for whom he may mourn.

themes: for his sister a virgin, that is nigh unto him
which hath had no husband
mercy to the sister left in his care

schools.summary: Gill: rejects the readings of a noble or a husband and interprets it of a priest, who was not to defile himself for kin more remote (such as those related by marriage), being a chief man among his people, lest he profane himself.

themes: he shall not defile himself, being a chief man
to profane himself
holiness fixing a limit on grief

schools.summary: Gill: baldness, shaving the beard, and cuttings were superstitious heathen mourning customs (Chaldeans, Greeks, Egyptians); forbidden to all Israel (Lev. 19:27-28), the more forbidden the priests.

themes: They shall not make baldness upon their head
nor make any cuttings in their flesh
the priest free of heathen despair

schools.summary: Gill: the priests, wholly given to God's service, should be clean and pure who bear the vessels of the Lord and set an example of holiness to others.
Ellicott: this is the reason they may not disfigure themselves — being sacred to the Lord, they are not to indulge in heathen manifestations of grief.

themes: They shall be holy unto their God
the bread of their God, they do offer
holy servants for the holy service (2 Tim. 2:21)

schools.summary: Gill: distinguishes the 'whore' who prostitutes herself from the 'profane' whose chastity was violated (forced or enticed); the divorced woman also forbidden the priest, that his marriage match his holiness.

themes: They shall not take a wife that is a whore, or profane
nor a woman put away from her husband
the priest's household to be without reproach

schools.summary: Gill: 'sanctify him' means to think and speak well of him, esteem him holy, and (for the magistrate) to use authority to keep the priesthood from unlawful marriages; grounded in the LORD who sanctifies them and is himself holy.

themes: Thou shalt sanctify him... he offereth the bread of thy God
he shall be holy unto thee
the LORD which sanctify you, am holy (Lev. 20:8)

schools.summary: Gill: the law applies whether she were betrothed or married; the burning was a more terrible death than the stoning of others guilty of the like, because she profaned her father.
Keil & Delitzsch: the daughter's whoredom profaned her father, whose holy office her sin disgraced.

themes: the daughter of any priest, if she profane herself
she profaneth her father: she shall be burnt with fire
the closest watch over those nearest the altar

schools.summary: Gill: the high priest is greater than his brethren, set above them in office, the anointing oil poured on his head; he was to be greatest in dignity.
Keil & Delitzsch: consecrated by the anointing oil and the holy garments, he was bound to a holiness above the rest, not to uncover his head nor rend his clothes.

themes: he that is the high priest... upon whose head the anointing oil was poured
shall not uncover his head, nor rend his clothes
the High Priest whose holiness no sorrow stains (Heb. 7:26)

schools.summary: Gill: he was not to enter a house where a dead body lay, lest he be unclean seven days and hindered from his office; the heathen later aped this in the priest of Jupiter.
Keil & Delitzsch: the high priest's separation was set above even the deepest natural ties, not defiling himself for father or mother.

themes: Neither shall he go in to any dead body
nor defile himself for his father, or for his mother
consecration overruling the closest claims of nature

schools.summary: Gill: the high priest was not to go out of the sanctuary in the time of service to follow the dead; the law respects him in the case of his dead; the crown of the anointing oil marks him set wholly apart, his honour to remain in the sanctuary.

themes: Neither shall he go out of the sanctuary
the crown of the anointing oil of his God is upon him
Christ's abiding ministry in the sanctuary (Heb. 7:25)

schools.summary: Gill: the high priest might take but one wife ('a wife,' one and not two), though polygamy was practised by others and even by common priests; she was to be a virgin, never known nor betrothed to another, answering the charge that the minister be the husband of one wife (1 Tim. 3:2).

themes: he shall take a wife in her virginity
one wife only, a virgin
the type of the Mediator unstained (1 Tim. 3:2)

schools.summary: Gill: the high priest might not marry even a widow, whether of a priest or an Israelite, nor one betrothed and left a widow; the rule was stricter than for the common priest, who might marry a widow — the high priest to take only a virgin of his own people.

themes: A widow, or a divorced woman, or profane, or an harlot... shall he not take
he shall take a virgin of his own people
the high priest's domestic purity, strictest of all

schools.summary: Gill: by such marriages his children would lie under disgrace, unfit to succeed him in the priesthood, or a disparagement to it.
Ellicott: he is not to contract any forbidden marriage, lest the children of such an issue, like their mother, be debarred the privileges of the priesthood and its portions.

themes: Neither shall he profane his seed among his people
for I the LORD do sanctify him
the holiness of the priesthood transmitted unbroken

schools.summary: Gill: after the holiness of the priests as to defilement and marriage, God adds laws concerning blemishes in their bodies that rendered them unfit for service.

Keil & Delitzsch: as the spiritual nature is reflected in the bodily form, only a faultless body could answer to the holiness of the priest, as even the Greeks and Romans required their priests to be whole and unmaimed.

themes: the LORD spake unto Moses
the law of bodily blemishes
outward soundness a parable of inward perfection

schools.summary: Gill: Aaron, as high priest, was to see the rules observed and examine who might serve; the blemished were not to approach to offer.
Keil & Delitzsch: the general law of vv.17-23 — whoever of Aaron's seed had any blemish was not to approach to offer the bread of his God.

themes: Whosoever... that hath any blemish, let him not approach
to offer the bread of his God
the one perfect Priest without blemish (Heb. 9:14)

schools.summary: Gill: the bar held whether the blemish were fixed from birth or transient, while it remained on him.
Clarke: the 'blind' is blind in one eye, for the wholly blind could not be employed in the service at all; the flat nose and the rest likewise unfitted for the altar.

themes: a blind man, or a lame, or... a flat nose, or any thing superfluous
the blemished not to approach
the priest's wholeness a sign of soundness in God's service

schools.summary: Gill: any bone or joint of hand or foot broken or distorted — clubfooted or crook-fingered — unfit to ascend the altar and lay the sacrifice in order; an emblem of those disorderly in walk and unfit for every good work.
Clarke and Ellicott: a badly cured fracture or dislocation of foot or hand barred the priest from service.

themes: a man that is brokenfooted, or brokenhanded
unfit to ascend the altar
whole in conduct as in body

schools.summary: Gill: 'crookbackt' is one with a hunch; notes the Jewish glosses reading the eye and brow defects among the blemishes.
Keil & Delitzsch: gathers the list of bodily imperfections — crookbackt, dwarf, blemish in the eye, scurvy, scabbed, maimed — that unfitted a priest for the altar.

themes: crookbackt, or a dwarf, or... a blemish in his eye
scurvy, or scabbed
the spotless Christ answering the law's whole demand

schools.summary: Gill: the Jewish writers reckon the blemishes to one hundred and forty, any one of which barred a priest from offering.
Keil & Delitzsch: no man with a blemish of the seed of Aaron, high or common, was to come nigh to offer the offerings of the LORD made by fire.

themes: No man that hath a blemish... shall come nigh to offer
he hath a blemish; he shall not come nigh
the altar belonging to the unblemished

schools.summary: Gill: their natural infirmities did not make them impure, morally or ceremonially, so they might eat of the sacrifices that impure persons might not; being priests with no inheritance, provision was made that they not perish for defects not of their own making.

themes: He shall eat the bread of his God
both of the most holy, and of the holy
the weak still owned and provided for by God

schools.summary: Gill: he might not enter the holy place before the veil to set the shewbread, light the lamps, or offer incense, nor come nigh the altar, though he might eat the bread.
Keil & Delitzsch: he was barred from the veil and the altar because of his blemish, that he profane not the sanctuaries, for the LORD sanctifies them.

themes: he shall not go in unto the vail, nor come nigh unto the altar
that he profane not my sanctuaries
holiness and mercy meeting

schools.summary: Gill: Moses told Aaron, his sons, and all Israel concerning defilement for the dead, marriages, and blemishes, that the priests be careful and the people know who was fit to put their sacrifices into the hands of.

themes: Moses told it unto Aaron, and to his sons, and unto all the children of Israel
priests and people alike charged
the holiness of the altar the whole nation's concern
